questions.prompt: Organic traffic just dropped 50% overnight. What should you check FIRST?

choices.text: Roll back all content changes from the last quarter.

Check for a technical regression: was there a recent deploy? Is robots.txt healthy? Are pages returning 200? Did a noindex tag accidentally roll out? Search Console's Page Indexing report is the fastest way to spot a site-wide indexing event.

questions.explanation: Always check technical regressions first. They're the fastest to find, the most often the cause of sudden drops, and the most damaging if left unfixed. A staging robots.txt deployed to production, an accidental noindex tag, a broken deploy — these are the most common causes of sudden 50% drops. Rule them out before assuming algorithm or content issues.

questions.prompt: Your traffic dropped on the same day as a Google core update. A consultant says: 'Roll back everything you did last month — that's what triggered it.' Is this good advice?

choices.text: Yes, always roll back recent changes after an update.

Usually wrong. Core updates change how Google evaluates content — not how it evaluates *your specific recent changes*. Reverting good work won't help and may hurt. The right move is to audit affected pages for content quality (Module 2's E-E-A-T, Who/How/Why, commodity vs non-commodity) and substantively improve them. Recovery takes 3–6 months minimum.
Only roll back changes made in the last 48 hours.

questions.explanation: Core updates change Google's evaluation framework — they shift which content quality signals matter more or less. They don't punish your specific recent changes. Panic-reverting good work doesn't help. The fix is content quality investment, and recovery is typically a 3–6 month timeline. Sometimes you need to wait for the next core update for recovery to fully land.
